name: Chamomile
summary: Chamomile is a traditional botanical most often used for sleep, relaxation, mild anxiety, and digestive comfort. A small randomized trial points to modest benefit for generalized anxiety, while evidence for sleep and digestion is more limited and lower-certainty, so effects are best framed as gentle rather than dramatic. It is generally well tolerated; people with ragweed-family (Asteraceae) allergies should be cautious, it may add to the effects of sedatives, and pregnancy warrants caution — discuss use with a clinician.

safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated for most users.

claimMap.claim: v9.2: Chamomile has RCT evidence for generalized anxiety symptoms; sleep use should remain secondary/traditional unless direct sleep evidence is added.
claimMap.notes: v9.2: Chamomile has RCT evidence for generalized anxiety symptoms; sleep use should remain secondary/traditional unless direct sleep evidence is added.

qualifiers.dose_or_duration: Chamomile extract 220–1500 mg/day in GAD trials; tea dosing is product-dependent

safety: Generally well tolerated for most users.

sources.title: PubMed PMID 19593179. Minimal citation row added from existing workbook PMID; title/year/journal still require PubMed metadata extraction.
sources.citation: PubMed PMID 19593179. Minimal citation row added from existing workbook PMID; title/year/journal still require PubMed metadata extraction.
sources.note: evidence citation lookup; PMID source present in Evidence_Register

contraindications: Known ragweed or Asteraceae/Compositae family allergy (cross-reactive allergic reactions including rare anaphylaxis)
pregnancy or breastfeeding without clinician guidance (traditional uterine-stimulant use)
bleeding disorders or anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy without clinician review (coumarin-like constituents may add to bleeding risk)
May potentiate sedatives or alcohol or benzodiazepines through additive CNS depression
case reports describe increased warfarin effect (elevated INR) with chronic chamomile tea use
theoretical CYP3A4 and CYP1A2 interactions with hepatically metabolized drugs.
